summary: Jeff Williams, Apple's 62-year-old COO, is retiring, with longtime operations lieutenant Sabih Khan taking over the COO role. While the operations transition is characteristically smooth, the more intriguing story is what happens to Apple's design teams, which have reported to Williams since Jony Ive's 2019 departure. Gruber argues Williams oversaw but never truly led design, leaving no stylistic fingerprint on Apple's products over six years. With design temporarily reporting to Tim Cook (who is 64), Apple still has no clear answer for who will provide the taste and design vision the company needs going forward.
key_points: The COO transition from Williams to Khan is orderly and reflects Apple's operationally excellent culture, with Khan having been groomed for the role since 2019.
Williams had no design background, and his role overseeing design since Ive's departure was managerial rather than creative — he asked questions but didn't push back or offer opinions.
Six years after Ive left, Apple has made no visible move toward naming a new chief design officer or SVP of design, which Gruber views as a growing problem.
Gruber suggests Ive's consolidation of hardware and software design under one roof may have made him harder to replace than Steve Jobs himself.
The transition leaves it less clear than ever whose taste is steering Apple's future product direction.
money_quotes: Apple's operations aren't just world-class, they're almost certainly world-best. Even their leadership transitions are operationally smooth.
I've long found it curious, if not downright dubious, that Apple's design leaders have reported to Williams ever since it was announced in 2019.
Apple's design teams reporting to an operations executive makes no more sense than it would for Apple's operations teams to report to, say, Alan Dye.
Looking back at the last six years of Apple design — hardware, software, packaging, architecture — I detect not one fingerprint of style or taste that belongs to Jeff Williams.
But it's a problem for the company if its products don't ultimately have a distinctive voice.
It might have ultimately made Ive more difficult to replace than Steve Jobs.
Six years after Jony Ive's departure, today's announcements leave it less clear than ever whose taste, ultimately, is steering the work of the company into the future.
key_insight: Apple's smooth COO succession masks a deeper unresolved problem: six years after Jony Ive's departure, the company still has no identifiable design leader whose taste shapes its products.
